Driven mad by the 'little mouse' signing up for Email!
I wanted my own Email account
There was one elementary-school computer class I still remember vividly. The teacher told us to each sign up for our own email and send him a message.
"Today's homework: go to Yahoo!, sign up for your own email, and send me a message. Content can be anything. My email is here..." the teacher said before the bell.
Honestly, I panicked when I heard the assignment, because I never paid attention in computer class — I was always playing on "
". I vaguely remember the teacher covering email signup, but I'd zoned out for sure.
When I got home, my first move was to call a classmate who actually paid attention, and ask them how to sign up.
"Beep beep..." rang a few times, then the line picked up.
"Hey~ is this XXX? It's Ray." I said.
"Oh! Ray, what's up?" my classmate asked.
"I want to ask how to sign up for a Yahoo email — it's the homework." I'd actually gone to my classmate's house to use their computer, since mine had already broken.
"Oh, that's easy! Open Yahoo!, click sign up, fill in your info — done, you have an email~" my classmate said.
Sounds simple, right? Brutal for me — I only knew how to search for
"Hey! XXX, I filled in all the fields, but there's one called 'backup email' — what do I put?" I asked.
"Backup email — just put one of someone's emails. Put my sister's: a0..." Here my classmate said something I didn't understand — "
"What was that symbol? How do I type it?" I asked.
"It's the 'little mouse'! Type 'little mouse'!" classmate said.
"Doesn't work! It's saying my email is wrong!" I looked at the email he gave me again to double-check.
"It says my email is invalid, please check. I'll read it again: a0... little mouse..."
A few rounds later, my classmate seemed to realize something:
"Wait, you're not typing 中文 'little mouse' literally are you?"
"Yeah! Aren't I supposed to?" I was confused.
"NO! Press Shift + 2! The symbol is called 'at' (little mouse)!" classmate explained.

After all that, I realized I'd been typing wrong. Just signing up for a Yahoo email took me nearly an hour. Once signed up, I figured out the rest by trial and error, finally sent the email to the teacher, and finished the assignment. (Pay attention in class, kids~~)
Earlier we covered LANs. To pass data or messages within the same network range, devices must be able to communicate within that range.
But if you want to go beyond your LAN — exchange data with computers on other LANs — what then?
Enter Email. It lets us pass data between different LANs. For example, I can send an email from a Taiwan computer to a US computer, and they'll receive it.
Early on there was no Line, no instant messengers — Email was how people sent messages and data.
But... have you ever wondered where Email came from?
Email was invented by Ray Tomlinson. In 1971 he designed the system that let people send letters between different computers to different users — kicking off the era of modern email.
When Ray Tomlinson invented Email, he needed a way to separate "username" from "host name", and chose "

" as the separator. At the time, "@" had basically no specific use or meaning — perfect for a separator. As the internet and DNS evolved, the original "host name" portion gradually became "domain name" identifying the email provider.
DNS (Domain Name System) is a system that converts domain names into IP addresses. So when you type
into the browser, that maps to a specific IP behind the scenes.
This format cleanly separates "username" and "domain". Some examples:
You can see how username and domain are separated by "
". This way you can clearly tell who the letter is for and which service is delivering it. So the Email format isn't random — it has history and meaning behind it.
" isn't limited to Email either — social platforms use it to tag users too, e.g.:
This tag has meaning too: "I'm mentioning a user named
on this platform / service" — and the concept is an extension of how Email uses it.
So when we send a letter from
, in plain language: "I'm on the gmail.com domain, sending a letter to a user named
With that, you should now understand why Email format is designed this way, and why social platforms use "
" originally separated username from host name, and in modern times became username vs domain name.
What was the "host name" (hostname) back then? Simply put — the computer's name. E.g., my username is
. That was the old way to identify a user on a particular host.

This separation distinguishes users from hosts because the same host can have multiple users, like creating different user accounts on Windows or macOS — each has its own settings and data while sharing the same computer:
When passing data on LAN, we identify user and host this way.
As the internet expanded, we stopped relying on host names alone, and introduced "domain name". Domain names clearly identify the email provider, letting Email cross different networks.
So if I have a Gmail account and my username is
You might wonder why it's not
. Reason: Gmail is Google's email service, but Gmail's domain is
Through the domain-name concept, we can see clearly which provider this letter goes to. Provider names usually use "domain names" to distinguish themselves:
These are all domain names — and through domain names, we know which provider a letter is heading to.
Quick summary of the era differences:
— identifies which user on which computer.
— identifies which user on which email provider.
Domain names have a lot more depth, but we won't go further here. Just understand domain names distinguish providers. Later chapters will go deeper.
